name: Dinka

sources.author: Remijsen, Bert & Gilley, Leoma
sources.title: Why are three-level vowel length systems rare? Insights from Dinka (Luanyjang dialect)
sources.notes: On the length/voice-quality/tone system the orthography under-marks.

sources.author: Moran, Steven & McCloy, Daniel (eds.)
sources.title: PHOIBLE 2.0
sources.publisher: Max Planck Institute for the Science of Human History
sources.notes: Attested phoneme inventory for glottocode dink1262.

notes: Dinka (Thuɔŋjäŋ) — Western Nilotic language of South Sudan, written in a Latin alphabet extended with ⟨ɛ ɔ ɣ ŋ⟩ and diaereses. THE GRAPHEME INVENTORY IS NOT YET SPECIFIED here (graphemes/allophones are empty). Dinka's phonology is exceptionally hard to map from spelling: it contrasts three vowel lengths, breathy vs. modal (creaky) voice, and lexical tone, and the orthography marks these only partially and inconsistently across the Agar/Bor/Padang/Rek standards. A grapheme map would need to commit to one dialect standard and to a documented convention for voice quality and tone. Glottolog: `din` is the Dinka macrolanguage, so `dink1262` is a family node by necessity. PHONEME INVENTORY: attested inventory from PHOIBLE (glottocode dink1262), declared directly. `graphemes` stays empty because none of that depends on the writing system: the sounds of this language are known regardless of whether — or how — it is written.

ancestors.notes: Nilotic — the classification node this language sits under.

location.notes: Glottolog's computed centroid for the Dinka family — a summary of a whole clade's range, not a place anyone speaks.
